questions.text: How are approved suppliers documented in PactoSigna?

options.text: In a single spreadsheet uploaded to the platform
options.explanation: PactoSigna uses structured supplier documents, not spreadsheets.

options.text: As supplier document types with structured frontmatter including name, category, status, and evaluation date
options.explanation: Per Section 5.3, suppliers are documented as supplier type documents in PactoSigna with structured frontmatter capturing supplier details.

options.text: In email records maintained by the purchasing department
options.explanation: Supplier records are managed as controlled documents in PactoSigna.

options.text: Suppliers are not tracked in PactoSigna
options.explanation: PactoSigna includes a supplier document type specifically for ASL management.

questions.text: How often must critical suppliers be re-evaluated?

options.explanation: Six months is more frequent than required for critical suppliers.
Per Section 5.7, critical suppliers are re-evaluated annually, while non-critical suppliers are re-evaluated biennially.
Biennial re-evaluation applies to non-critical suppliers, not critical ones.
Re-evaluation is scheduled regardless of nonconformances.

questions.text: PactoSigna captures a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for each release, providing a complete inventory of software components.
questions.explanation: Per Section 5.5, PactoSigna captures the SBOM for each release alongside the SOUP register.

questions.text: What must be verified when receiving a SOUP software component?

options.explanation: Multiple verification checks are required, not just the name.

options.text: Version, integrity check (checksums/signatures), license compliance, and security scan
options.explanation: Per Section 5.6, incoming verification for software components includes version verification, integrity checks, license compliance, and security scans.
License compliance is one of several verification steps required.
Developer certifications are not part of SOUP incoming verification.

questions.text: What factors determine the rigor of SOUP risk assessment per IEC 62304?

options.text: The cost of the software component
options.explanation: Cost is not a factor in IEC 62304 risk assessment.

options.text: The device's safety classification (Class A, B, or C)
options.explanation: Per Section 5.5, SOUP risk is assessed based on the device's IEC 62304 safety classification, with higher classes requiring more rigorous assessment.

options.text: The number of downloads the component has
options.explanation: Download count is not part of IEC 62304 risk assessment criteria.
Programming language is not a factor in IEC 62304 safety classification.

questions.text: What approval is required to purchase from a supplier not on the Approved Supplier List?

options.explanation: Non-ASL purchases require explicit approval with documentation.

options.text: Quality Manager approval with documented justification
options.explanation: Per Section 5.3, purchases from suppliers not on the ASL require Quality Manager approval with documented justification.
Quality Manager approval is sufficient; CEO approval is not specifically required.
Department head approval alone is not sufficient; Quality Manager approval is required.
